labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B4.2 Ecological niches > B4.2.13 Competitive exclusion and the uniqueness of ecological niches
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B4.2.13 Competitive exclusion and the uniqueness of ecological niches
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question describes species occupying distinct foraging zones to coexist without displacement. This is the definition of niche partitioning, which is the process that allows species to maintain the uniqueness of their ecological niches and avoid competitive exclusion as described in this topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B4.2 Ecological niches > B4.2.1 Ecological niche as the role of a species in an ecosystem
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B4.2.1 Ecological niche as the role of a species in an ecosystem
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The vertical foraging zones represent the specific 'role' or ecological niche of each bird species within the ecosystem, illustrating how different species occupy different niches in the same habitat.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B4.2 Ecological niches > B4.2.12 Fundamental and realized niches
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B4.2.12 Fundamental and realized niches
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Niche partitioning results in species occupying a 'realized niche' (their actual foraging zone) which is a specific portion of their 'fundamental niche' (the total potential area they could forage in if there were no competition).

options.content: Niche partitioning
options.markscheme: Niche partitioning (or resource partitioning) occurs when species divide resources (such as space or food) to avoid direct competition, allowing them to coexist in the same habitat. <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.content: Competitive exclusion
options.markscheme: Competitive exclusion is the principle that two species competing for the exact same resources cannot coexist; one will eventually eliminate the other. This is the opposite of the coexistence shown in the data.

options.content: Fundamental niche
options.markscheme: The fundamental niche refers to the entire range of conditions a species could potentially occupy in the absence of competition, rather than the act of dividing resources to reduce competition.

options.content: Ecological succession
options.markscheme: Succession is the process of change in the species structure of an ecological community over time, whereas this data describes a stable spatial arrangement between species.

questions.specification: Evolution by natural selection is a central unifying concept in biology. Answer the following questions about how the theory was developed.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.1 Natural selection > D4.1.1 Natural selection as the mechanism driving evolutionary change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.1.1 Natural selection as the mechanism driving evolutionary change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks for an outline of the theory of natural selection as the mechanism driving evolutionary change, which is the exact description of this leaf-level topic. The mention of inductive reasoning is a pedagogical context often integrated within the study of the development of this specific theory.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A4.1 Evolution and speciation > A4.1.1 Evolution as change in the heritable characteristics of a population
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A4.1.1 Evolution as change in the heritable characteristics of a population
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This topic covers the fundamental definition of evolution as a change in the heritable characteristics of a population, which is the central outcome of the natural selection process described in the question.

parts.content: Outline the theory of evolution by natural selection.
parts.markscheme:
- Organisms produce more offspring than can survive, leading to competition/struggle for existence <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Variation exists among individuals in a population <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Individuals with favourable traits are more likely to survive and reproduce (differential survival and reproduction) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Heritable favourable traits are passed to offspring, increasing in frequency over generations (leading to gradual change/adaptation) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how inductive reasoning contributed to the development of the theory of evolution by natural selection.
parts.markscheme:
- Darwin made specific observations of variation and patterns in nature (e.g. finch beak variations, fossil evidence, biogeography) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- He inferred that individuals with certain traits have higher survival/reproductive success based on these observations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- He generalised from multiple specific observations to propose a general mechanism (natural selection) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The theory was built from accumulated evidence rather than starting from a single assumed premise (i.e. inductive reasoning from evidence to theory) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how natural selection can lead to the formation of new species.
parts.markscheme:
- Variation exists within populations due to mutation and sexual reproduction (recombination) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Different selection pressures in different environments favour different alleles/phenotypes, causing divergence between populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Over time, allele frequency changes accumulate so populations become increasingly genetically different <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Barriers to gene flow arise (geographical, ecological, behavioural, temporal or mechanical isolation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Isolation reduces/interferes with interbreeding, preventing homogenisation of allele frequencies <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reproductive isolation becomes established (no fertile offspring/assortative mating), defining separate species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Natural selection can reinforce reproductive isolation (selection against hybrids where hybrids have lower fitness) leading to speciation <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
In a study of genetic linkage in tomato plants, a test cross was performed using a parent heterozygous for both stem colour and plant height ($BbTt$) and a parent with a brown stem and dwarf height ($bbtt$). The observed phenotypes of the offspring are recorded in the table below.

| Phenotype | Number of individuals |
| :--- | :--- |
| Black stem, Tall height | 230 |
| Brown stem, Dwarf height | 210 |
| Black stem, Dwarf height | 40 |
| Brown stem, Tall height | 20 |

Based on these results, what is the recombination frequency for these two genes?

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Recombinants are 40 + 20 = 60 out of 500, giving 12.0%.
Incorrect. Recombinants are 60 out of 500, which is 12.0%, not 10.0%.
Correct. Recombinants are the less frequent phenotypes: 40 (black dwarf) + 20 (brown tall) = 60. Total offspring = 230 + 210 + 40 + 20 = 500. Recombination frequency = (60/500) × 100 = 12.0%.
Incorrect. 24.0% would correspond to 120 recombinants out of 500, but there are 60.

The figure shows experimental evolution of microbial lines using mutation accumulation (MA).

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/57a5ac13-4d00-434a-b231-ccf91cafb9a4.jpeg)

- **Panel d**: Experimental workflow: A microbial progenitor with an $MSH2$ gene deletion ($msh2^-$) undergoes ~1500 generations of MA. Mutation rates are then quantified by two methods: a fluctuation test (I) and a second MA round with whole genome sequencing (II).

parts.content: Using Panel a, describe how the mutation rate affects natural selection and explain the tradeoff shown between beneficial and deleterious mutations.
parts.markscheme:
- High mutation rates increase genetic diversity (raw material for selection) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, high rates also increase the deleterious mutation load, which reduces the average fitness of the population <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the trends
steps.content:
First, let's look closely at **Panel a**. The graph shows two separate lines of best fit, both with positive slopes. This means that as the **mutation rate** (on the x-axis) increases, the frequency of both **beneficial** and **deleterious mutations** (on the y-axis) also increases. 

In IB Biology, we recognize mutation as a primary source of genetic variation, which is essential for evolution.

steps.title: Beneficial mutations and selection
steps.content:
From the red line in the graph, we see that a higher mutation rate leads to a higher occurrence of beneficial mutations. 

In the context of **natural selection**, these beneficial mutations provide the 'raw material' for the process. They create new alleles that may provide a survival advantage, allowing the population to adapt to its environment over time. 

This concept is central to **Topic D4.1: Natural Selection**, which states that mutation is one of the main sources of the variation required for selection to occur.

steps.title: The cost of mutations
steps.content:
However, the blue line in **Panel a** shows the 'dark side' of this increase. As the mutation rate rises, the frequency of **deleterious mutations** increases as well—and usually at a much higher rate than beneficial ones.

These harmful mutations create a **deleterious mutation load**. This means that while a few individuals might get lucky with a beneficial trait, many more will inherit mutations that reduce their fitness (their ability to survive and reproduce).

steps.title: Explaining the tradeoff
steps.content:
The **tradeoff** shown in the graph is the balance between adaptation and damage:

1. **The Benefit:** A high mutation rate increases genetic diversity, giving natural selection more beneficial variants to 'choose' from for adaptation.
2. **The Cost:** Simultaneously, it increases the number of harmful mutations, which lowers the average fitness of the population as a whole.

To earn full marks, you must mention both the increase in diversity (raw material) and the reduction in average fitness (deleterious load).

steps.title: Analyze Panel A Trends
steps.content: In Panel a, we observe that as the mutation rate increases, both beneficial and deleterious mutations occur more frequently. However, the curve for deleterious mutations rises significantly, indicating that the vast majority of new mutations are harmful rather than helpful.

steps.title: Mutation as Raw Material
steps.content:
From an evolutionary perspective, a higher mutation rate increases **genetic diversity**. This diversity serves as the 'raw material' for natural selection; without it, there would be no variation for selection to act upon to improve adaptation to the environment.

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic D4.1: Natural Selection**.

steps.title: Explain the Mutational Load
steps.content:
The tradeoff shown in the graph is that while high mutation rates provide more opportunities for beneficial changes, they simultaneously increase the **mutational load**. 

Because deleterious mutations are far more common, a high mutation rate leads to an accumulation of harmful alleles that reduces the overall average fitness of the population.

steps.title: Summary of Tradeoff
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, ensure you mention both sides of the balance:
1.  **Diversity:** High mutation rates increase the raw material (variation) available for natural selection.
2.  **Fitness Cost:** High mutation rates increase the deleterious load, which lowers population fitness.

parts.content: Explain how the loss of the $MSH2$ gene affects mutation rates in the microbial lines, using evidence from Panels b and c.
parts.markscheme:
- $msh2^-$ lines show significantly higher mutation rates (as seen in Panel b) because the loss of the gene prevents effective DNA mismatch repair <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Evidence from Panel c shows that reintroducing functional $MSH2$ restores a lower mutation rate, confirming its role in maintaining genome stability <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the impact in Panel b
steps.content:
First, we look at **Panel b**, which shows the mutation rates for microbial lines labeled $msh2^-$. This label indicates that the mismatch repair gene $MSH2$ has been deleted. 

In the graph, you can see that these $msh2^-$ lines exhibit **significantly higher mutation rates** compared to baseline. Because $MSH2$ is a mismatch repair gene, its absence means that errors occurring during DNA replication are not corrected, leading to an accumulation of mutations. This helps us earn the first mark by identifying that the loss of the gene prevents effective DNA repair.

highlights.text: Mutation rates across multiple MA lines lacking the mismatch repair gene MSH2 (msh2-)

steps.title: Correlate with restoration in Panel c
steps.content:
Next, we examine **Panel c** to confirm our deduction. This panel shows the mutation rate "before and after" the $MSH2$ gene is put back into the system.

Notice the dramatic drop in the mutation rate in the **"Restore"** section of the graph. By reintroducing a functional $MSH2$ gene, the mutation rate returns to a much lower level. This evidence confirms that the $MSH2$ gene is directly responsible for maintaining genome stability by keeping mutation rates low.

highlights.text: Mutation rates in MA lines before and after the reintroduction of a functional MSH2 gene

steps.title: Synthesize the final explanation
steps.content:
To score full marks, your explanation should connect these two pieces of evidence:

1.  **Panel b evidence:** The $msh2^-$ lines show high mutation rates because the loss of the gene stops effective DNA mismatch repair.
2.  **Panel c evidence:** Reintroducing the functional gene restores a lower mutation rate, which proves that $MSH2$ is necessary for DNA repair and genomic stability.

parts.content: Suggest how variation generated in this experiment could affect the survival of some microbial lines under antibiotic stress.
parts.markscheme:
- Beneficial mutations (e.g., antibiotic resistance alleles) may arise by chance, allowing survival <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conversely, a high deleterious load could reduce overall fitness, making those lines more vulnerable to stress <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Variation and Mutation Rate
steps.content: In this experiment, the microbial lines lack the $MSH2$ gene ($msh2^-$), which is responsible for DNA mismatch repair. Without this repair mechanism, the mutation rate increases significantly. This high mutation rate generates a large pool of **genetic variation** within the population, which is the raw material for evolutionary adaptation.

steps.title: Acquiring Beneficial Resistance Alleles
steps.content:
From an **Evolutionary Adaptation Approach**, high genetic variation increases the statistical probability that a beneficial mutation will occur by chance. In an environment with antibiotic stress, a specific mutation might arise that functions as an **antibiotic resistance allele**. 

Microbial lines that happen to acquire these beneficial alleles will have a massive survival advantage under the selective pressure of the antibiotic, allowing them to survive and reproduce while others die off.

steps.title: Impact of Deleterious Load
steps.content:
However, mutation is random, and most mutations are neutral or harmful rather than beneficial. Because the $msh2^-$ lines accumulate mutations rapidly, they may develop a high **deleterious load** (an accumulation of harmful mutations). 

This load can reduce the overall biological fitness of the line, making those specific microbes weaker and more vulnerable to environmental stresses, including antibiotics, even if they haven't encountered a lethal dose yet.

steps.title: Summary of Survival Outcomes
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, your answer should highlight both the "lucky" chance of survival and the risk of accumulated weakness:

1.  **Survival**: Beneficial mutations (like resistance alleles) arise by chance, enabling survival under antibiotic pressure.
2.  **Vulnerability**: A high deleterious load reduces fitness, making some lines more likely to fail under stress.

This concept is a key part of **Topic D4.1 (Natural Selection)** and **Topic C3.2 (Defense against disease)** in your IB Biology syllabus.

steps.title: Understand mutation and variation
steps.content: In this experiment, the deletion of the $MSH2$ gene (the $msh2^-$ lines) significantly increases the mutation rate. This process generates a vast amount of genetic variation. As covered in **Topic D1.3 (Mutations)**, mutations are structural changes to genes and are the primary source of genetic variation in a population. When we introduce a stressor like an antibiotic, this variation determines which lines live and which die.

steps.title: Identify potential beneficial mutations
steps.content:
The first way variation affects survival is through the rare chance of a "lucky" mutation. In a high-mutation environment, there is a statistical possibility that a mutation will occur in a gene that provides antibiotic resistance (e.g., a mutation that changes a protein pump to export the drug). 

If a line develops a beneficial mutation like an **antibiotic resistance allele**, it will be able to survive and reproduce while other lines perish. This is the basis of natural selection discussed in **Topic D4.1**.

highlights.text: Beneficial mutations (e.g., antibiotic resistance alleles) may arise by chance, allowing survival

steps.title: Apply the Genetic Load Approach
steps.content:
While beneficial mutations are possible, the **Genetic Load Approach** focuses on the more common outcome: **deleterious mutations**. Because mutations are random, the majority of them are harmful or reduce the efficiency of the cell's metabolism and growth.

In lines with a high mutation rate, these harmful mutations accumulate over time, creating a heavy **genetic load**. This reduces the overall "fitness" or resilience of the microbial line. When these already-weakened lines are hit with antibiotic stress, they are much more likely to die because they lack the biological robustness to survive even low levels of toxicity.

highlights.text: Conversely, a high deleterious load could reduce overall fitness, making those lines more vulnerable to stress

steps.title: Summarize the two-fold effect
steps.content:
To earn both marks for this question, you must mention both sides of the coin:

1.  **Survival via luck**: Some lines survive because they randomly develop beneficial resistance alleles.
2.  **Death via load**: Other lines perish because their high genetic load (accumulation of harmful mutations) has made them too weak to withstand the added stress of the antibiotic.

parts.content: State how experiments like this help us understand natural selection as a continuous evolutionary process.
parts.markscheme: Mutation accumulation (MA) experiments allow researchers to observe real-time adaptation and the ongoing balance between mutation and selection <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify experimental scale
steps.content: To understand how this experiment illustrates evolution, we first look at the timescale. The workflow shows that the microbes undergo approximately **1500 generations**. For microorganisms, this represents a significant evolutionary period compressed into a human-observable timeframe.

steps.title: Observe real-time adaptation
steps.content: Because we can monitor these 1500 generations from start to finish, researchers can observe **real-time adaptation**. Unlike looking at fossils, which show snapshots of the past, this approach allows us to see beneficial mutations arise and spread through a population as they happen.

steps.title: Evaluate the evolutionary balance
steps.content: These experiments demonstrate that natural selection is a continuous process by showing the **ongoing balance between mutation and selection**. While mutations constantly generate new genetic variation (some deleterious, some beneficial), natural selection continually acts on that variation to shape the population's fitness over time.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: In summary, experiments like this allow us to witness the dynamic and continuous nature of evolution by providing a direct view of how populations adapt to their environment over many generations.

steps.title: Continuous variation through mutation
steps.content: Experiments like this show that genetic variation is not a one-time event but a continuous process. By using a **microbial progenitor with an $MSH2$ gene deletion ($msh2^-$)**, researchers increase the mutation rate, allowing them to observe the constant introduction of new alleles into the gene pool over thousands of generations.

highlights.text: microbial progenitor with an MSH2 gene deletion (msh2-)

steps.title: Observing real-time selection pressures
steps.content: Because the experiment follows the population for **~1500 generations**, we can see how the environment acts on the variation provided by mutation. In the absence of strong repair mechanisms, mutations accumulate rapidly, providing the 'raw material' that natural selection can then 'sort' or filter based on which traits improve survival and reproduction.

steps.title: The mutation-selection balance
steps.content:
This study helps us understand the **ongoing balance between mutation and selection**. While mutation continuously adds potentially harmful or beneficial changes (as seen in Panel a), selection pressures constantly remove the deleterious ones and promote the beneficial ones. This dynamic interaction ensures that evolution is a continuous, real-time process of adaptation.

To understand more about how variation is produced, you can review **Topic D4.1: Natural Selection** in your syllabus.

<note>Accept any value in the range $2-2.1\%$.</note>

steps.title: Identify the highest bar
steps.content: To find the region with the **highest percentage**, we first look at the three bars in Figure A. By visually comparing their heights, we can see that the middle bar, labeled **Segmental duplications**, is clearly taller than both the "Tandem repeats" and "Reference regions" bars.

steps.title: Analyze the y-axis scale
steps.content: Next, we look at the y-axis, which is labeled "Variants deviating from HWE (%)". The scale has major markings at $0$, $1.0$, and $2.0$. Each major unit is divided into smaller increments of $0.1$ or $0.2$, depending on the grid lines available.

steps.title: Estimate the percentage value
steps.content:
Now, trace the top of the **Segmental duplications** bar horizontally to the left toward the y-axis. 

We can see that the top of the bar sits just slightly above the line for $2.0\%$. Since it is a very small distance above that line, we can estimate this value to be approximately $2.1\%$.

steps.title: Final answer and marking
steps.content:
The region is **Segmental duplication** and the approximate percentage is **$2.1\%$**.

In the IB markscheme, you would earn **1 mark** for correctly identifying both the region and a percentage within the range of $2.0\%$ to $2.1\%$.

parts.content: Explain what heterozygote excess means in terms of genotype frequencies relative to Hardy-Weinberg predictions.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that the observed heterozygote frequency ($2pq$) is higher than predicted by the Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** that more individuals in the population carry two different alleles than would be expected under random mating <marks>A1</marks>
- **Suggest** that this suggests a selective advantage for heterozygotes (heterozygote advantage) or disassortative mating <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the Hardy-Weinberg Prediction
steps.content:
To understand any deviation from equilibrium, we first look at the **Hardy-Weinberg equation**. This model predicts the frequency of genotypes in a population where no evolution is occurring.

In the equation:
$$p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$$

The term $2pq$ represents the **predicted frequency of heterozygotes** based on the allele frequencies ($p$ and $q$) in the gene pool. Under normal equilibrium conditions, the number of heterozygotes we see in a population should match this mathematical prediction.

steps.title: Compare Observed vs. Predicted
steps.content:
Using the **Frequency Comparison Approach**, "heterozygote excess" is defined by a specific mathematical inequality. It means that the **observed frequency** of heterozygotes (the actual number of individuals with two different alleles found in the population) is **significantly higher** than the frequency predicted by the $2pq$ term.

In simple terms:
$$\text{Observed Heterozygotes} > 2pq$$

steps.title: Interpret the Population Data
steps.content:
When we observe this excess, it tells us that more individuals in the population carry two different alleles at a specific locus than would be expected if mating were purely random. 

This is the opposite of "heterozygote deficiency" (or homozygote excess), and it signals that the population is **not** in Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium for that specific trait.

steps.title: Identify Biological Causes
steps.content:
Why would this happen? In IB Biology, we look for biological reasons that violate HWE assumptions:

1.  **Heterozygote Advantage (Overdominance):** Individuals with the heterozygous genotype have a higher fitness (better survival or reproduction) than either homozygote. A classic example is Sickle Cell Trait in malaria-prone regions.
2.  **Disassortative Mating:** Individuals preferentially choose mates with different phenotypes/genotypes than themselves, increasing the variety of alleles in offspring.

According to the markscheme, stating that the observed frequency is higher than predicted earns **1 mark**, explaining that more individuals carry different alleles than expected earns **1 mark**, and suggesting a selective advantage or non-random mating earns the final **1 mark**.

steps.title: Define the HWE expectation
steps.content:
To understand "excess," we first need to know what is expected. In a population at Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium, we use the equation:

$$p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$$

In this model, the term **$2pq$** represents the predicted frequency of heterozygotes (individuals with two different alleles) based purely on the allele frequencies $p$ and $q$. If you need a refresher on these calculations, check out **Topic D4.1: Natural Selection** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Compare observed to predicted
steps.content:
Heterozygote excess occurs when the **observed frequency** of heterozygotes in a real-world population is significantly **higher** than the $2pq$ value predicted by the Hardy-Weinberg equation.

This means that when we look at the actual genetic data (like the variants in Figure A), we find more individuals with a "mixed" genotype than we would expect to see if alleles were being handed out by pure chance.

highlights.text: observed heterozygote frequency ($2pq$) is higher than predicted

steps.title: Identify biological violations
steps.content:
From a mechanistic perspective, HWE only holds true if several assumptions are met (like random mating and no natural selection). An excess tells us these assumptions are being **violated**.

There are two main biological reasons for this:
1.  **Heterozygote Advantage:** Natural selection is actively favoring individuals with two different alleles, allowing them to survive and reproduce better than homozygotes.
2.  **Disassortative Mating:** Individuals are specifically choosing mates that have different genotypes from their own, rather than mating randomly.

highlights.text: suggests a selective advantage for heterozygotes (heterozygote advantage) or disassortative mating

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To secure all 3 marks on an IB exam, ensure you mention:
*   The observed frequency is higher than the predicted $2pq$ <marks>A1</marks>
*   There are more individuals with two different alleles than expected under random mating <marks>A1</marks>
*   The cause is likely **heterozygote advantage** or **disassortative mating** <marks>R1</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the expected heterozygote frequency ($2pq$) if the allele frequencies in a population are $p = 0.6$ and $q = 0.4$. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- **Substitute** the allele frequencies into the formula for heterozygote frequency: $2 \times 0.6 \times 0.4$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the result: $0.48$ (or $48\%$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given allele frequencies
steps.content:
From the question, we are provided with the following allele frequencies:
- The frequency of the first allele ($p$) is $0.6$.
- The frequency of the second allele ($q$) is $0.4$.

steps.title: Identify the heterozygote frequency formula
steps.content:
According to the **Hardy–Weinberg principle** (covered in **Topic D4.1**), the frequency of the heterozygote genotype in a population is represented by the term $2pq$ in the equation:

$$p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$$

Our goal is to calculate this $2pq$ term directly using the values provided.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
To find the expected frequency, we substitute the values of $p$ and $q$ into the formula:

$$\text{Heterozygote frequency} = 2 \times p \times q$$

$$\text{Heterozygote frequency} = 2 \times 0.6 \times 0.4$$

This substitution earns you the first mark (**M1**).

gdc.instructions: Type 2 × 0.6 × 0.4 in the calculation menu and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: On the home screen, type 2 * 0.6 * 0.4 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the frequency by multiplying the constant 2 by the two allele frequencies.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
Performing the multiplication:

$$\begin{aligned} 2 \times 0.6 \times 0.4 &= 1.2 \times 0.4 \\ &= 0.48 \end{aligned}$$

The expected heterozygote frequency is **0.48** (or **48%**). Providing this final value earns the second mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify given allele frequencies
steps.content:
First, let's look at the allele frequencies provided in the question. These concepts are part of **Topic D4.1: Natural Selection** in your IB Biology syllabus.

We are given:
- The frequency of the dominant allele: $p = 0.6$
- The frequency of the recessive allele: $q = 0.4$

To find the frequency of the heterozygotes ($2pq$), we will use the relationship where the sum of all genotype frequencies equals 1:  
$$p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$$

steps.title: Calculate homozygous frequencies
steps.content:
Using our specific approach, we first calculate the frequencies of the two homozygous genotypes:

1.  **Homozygous dominant ($p^2$):**
    $$p^2 = (0.6)^2 = 0.36$$

2.  **Homozygous recessive ($q^2$):**
    $$q^2 = (0.4)^2 = 0.16$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.6 and press the [x²] key, then press [ENTER]. Repeat for 0.4.

calculator.description: Calculate the squares of the allele frequencies to find homozygous frequencies.

steps.title: Solve for heterozygote frequency
steps.content:
Now, we find the remaining frequency, which represents the heterozygotes ($2pq$). We do this by subtracting the sum of the homozygous frequencies from 1:

$$\begin{aligned} 2pq &= 1 - (p^2 + q^2) \\ 2pq &= 1 - (0.36 + 0.16) \\ 2pq &= 1 - 0.52 \\ 2pq &= 0.48 \end{aligned}$$

In the IB markscheme, showing this substitution or the calculation steps earns you the first mark (**M1**).

gdc.instructions: Calculate 1 minus the sum of 0.36 and 0.16.

calculator.description: Subtract the sum of the homozygous frequencies from 1.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The expected heterozygote frequency ($2pq$) is **0.48** (or **48%**).

Final check: $0.36 + 0.48 + 0.16 = 1.00$. The math holds up! Stating this final value correctly earns you the second mark (**A1**).

Figure B shows the distribution of allele balance (AB) among variant carriers.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/S9xx4yc_besIiI8xGP7q8.jpeg)

From Figure B, estimate the total cumulative percentage of variant carriers with "Normal" allele balance ($0.4-0.55$) and explain why this metric is important for interpreting genetic data.
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** the total cumulative percentage for the specified range: $20.0\% + 23.4\% + 23.0\% = 66.4\%$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** that a normal allele balance (near $0.5$) confirms reliable heterozygote detection and reduces the risk of genotyping errors <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the relevant data
steps.content: To find the total cumulative percentage for the 'Normal' allele balance range ($0.4–0.55$), we need to look at Figure B and identify the percentage values for the three bins (bars) that fall within this interval.

steps.title: Sum the bin values
steps.content:
By observing the y-axis (Percentage of variant carriers) for the three bins between $0.40$ and $0.55$, we extract the following values:

*   **Bin 0.40–0.45:** $20.0\%$
*   **Bin 0.45–0.50:** $23.4\%$
*   **Bin 0.50–0.55:** $23.0\%$

Now, we add them together:

$$20.0\% + 23.4\% + 23.0\% = 66.4\%$$

gdc.instructions: Type 20.0 + 23.4 + 23.0 and press EXE or ENTER.

ti84.instructions: Enter 20.0 + 23.4 + 23.0 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: In a new expression line, type 20.0 + 23.4 + 23.0.

calculator.description: Calculate the total percentage for the 0.40 to 0.55 range.

steps.title: Interpret the genetic significance
steps.content:
In genetics (specifically **Topic D3.2: Inheritance**), an allele balance near $0.5$ is a critical quality control metric. 

Because a heterozygote has two different alleles ($Aa$), we expect roughly $50\%$ of the DNA sequencing reads to show one allele and $50\%$ to show the other. If the balance is significantly far from $0.5$, it might suggest a sequencing error, a duplication, or a contamination issue. Therefore, finding most variants near $0.5$ confirms **reliable heterozygote detection** and reduces the risk of **genotyping errors**.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content: The total cumulative percentage is **$66.4\%$**. This metric is important because a normal allele balance (near $0.5$) validates that the detected variants are true heterozygotes and ensures the accuracy of the genomic data.

parts.content: List two Hardy-Weinberg conditions that, if violated, could explain the observed deviation due to heterozygote excess in certain regions.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** any two conditions whose violation leads to heterozygote excess: <marks>A1A1</marks>
    - Non-random (disassortative) mating
    - Natural selection (heterozygote advantage or overdominance)
    - Migration (gene flow) bringing in individuals with different allele frequencies
    - Small population size resulting in random sampling error (genetic drift)

2 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark for each correct condition listed, up to a maximum of 2 marks.</note>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
The question asks for two **Hardy-Weinberg conditions** that, when violated, lead to a **heterozygote excess** (having more individuals with the $2pq$ genotype than predicted by the equilibrium). 

This topic is covered in **Topic D4.1: Natural Selection**, specifically looking at how allele frequencies change over time when certain assumptions aren't met.

steps.title: Violation: Natural Selection
steps.content:
One key condition for HWE is that there is **no natural selection**. If this is violated through **heterozygote advantage** (also known as **overdominance**), individuals with the heterozygous genotype have a higher fitness than either homozygote. 

Because these individuals survive and reproduce more successfully, the population will show a higher proportion of heterozygotes than the $2pq$ value calculated from the allele frequencies.

steps.title: Violation: Random Mating
steps.content:
Another condition is **random mating**. If mating is **non-random**, specifically **disassortative mating** (where individuals choose mates with different phenotypes or genotypes than their own), the frequency of heterozygotes in the next generation will increase. 

Essentially, "opposites attract" at the genetic level, which naturally produces more heterozygous offspring than random chance would predict.

steps.title: Other possible violations
steps.content:
While the two above are the most common explanations for an excess, other violations include:

*   **Migration (Gene Flow):** If a group of individuals from a population with very different allele frequencies moves in, the immediate mixing can result in more heterozygotes than expected.
*   **Small Population Size:** In very small populations, **genetic drift** can cause random fluctuations. While drift often decreases variation, a "sampling error" can occasionally result in an accidental excess of heterozygotes.

steps.title: Final selection
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks, you can list any two of these factors. The most standard answers for IB exams are:

1.  **Natural selection** (specifically heterozygote advantage)
2.  **Non-random mating** (specifically disassortative mating)

options.content: Stabilizing selection
options.markscheme: In stabilizing selection, the intermediate phenotype (moderate resistance) would become more frequent while both extremes would be selected against, which contradicts the observed increase in the high-resistance phenotype.

options.content: Disruptive selection
options.markscheme: In disruptive selection, both extreme phenotypes (low and high resistance) would be favored over the intermediate phenotype, resulting in a bimodal distribution rather than a shift toward a single extreme.

options.content: Directional selection
options.markscheme: Directional selection occurs when environmental changes favor one extreme phenotype over others. Here, the population shifts consistently toward high resistance as the pesticide acts as a selective pressure.

options.content: Sexual selection
options.markscheme: Sexual selection is based on traits that increase an individual's chance of mating. The scenario describes a survival advantage related to an external environmental factor (pesticide), which is characteristic of natural selection.

questions.specification:
The graph shows the population distribution of the medium ground finch (*Geospiza fortis*) on the island of Daphne Major before and after a severe drought. During the drought, small, soft seeds became scarce, while large, woody seeds remained available.

![finch population graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-f2105896-57b8-43b3-a28e-8e51b187cc74-1771929956947/55b28a24-a79d-4cd3-b60a-a0c4fe18d99d.jpeg)

Which statement best explains the change in the population distribution observed after the drought?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.1 Natural selection > D4.1.1 Natural selection as the mechanism driving evolutionary change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.1.1 Natural selection as the mechanism driving evolutionary change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the mechanism behind the change in population beak depth, which is natural selection driving evolutionary change.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.1 Natural selection > D4.1.12 Differences between directional, disruptive and stabilizing selection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.1.12 Differences between directional, disruptive and stabilizing selection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The graph specifically illustrates directional selection, where the mean phenotype of the population shifts toward one extreme (larger beaks) in response to environmental change.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.1 Natural selection > D4.1.4 Abiotic factors as selection pressures
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.1.4 Abiotic factors as selection pressures
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The drought is an abiotic factor that serves as the specific selection pressure mentioned in the prompt.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.1 Natural selection > D4.1.5 Differences between individuals in adaptation, survival and reproduction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.1.5 Differences between individuals in adaptation, survival and reproduction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question addresses how differences in beak size (adaptation) lead to differences in feeding efficiency (survival) and thus reproductive success.

options.content: The environmental stress of the drought caused individual birds to physically modify their beaks to suit the harder seeds.
options.markscheme: This is a Lamarckian misconception. Individuals do not adapt their physical structures during their lifetime to suit the environment; instead, evolutionary change occurs at the population level as those with favorable traits survive and reproduce.

options.content: Natural selection favored individuals with larger beak depths due to their increased feeding efficiency.
options.markscheme: Natural selection occurs when environmental changes (the drought) provide a selective advantage to individuals with specific variations (larger beak depths). The data shows that birds with larger beaks had a significantly higher survival rate because they could crack the remaining large seeds, leading to a shift in the population's characteristics.

options.content: The drought increased the frequency of mutation events in the population, resulting in new alleles for increased beak size.
options.markscheme: Mutations are random genetic events and are not 'triggered' or directed by the environmental needs of an organism.

options.content: Survival was independent of beak size, and the observed change was due to a random founder effect.
options.markscheme: The data clearly shows that survival was not random; there is a strong correlation between beak depth and the probability of survival, which is characteristic of natural selection rather than random genetic drift or the founder effect.

questions.specification: What reduces variation in a population?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.1 Natural selection > D4.1.1 Natural selection as the mechanism driving evolutionary change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.1.1 Natural selection as the mechanism driving evolutionary change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the process that reduces variation. Natural selection (option C) is the primary mechanism that reduces genetic variation by selecting for favorable traits and eliminating less adapted phenotypes.
